title: 11th Edition Is Fully Modeled — Come Check My Work
subtitle: Every damage-affecting rule in 11th edition is now in the math, and the edition-wide rankings are the proof.
summary: 11th edition's damage-affecting rules are now modeled end to end. The new edition-wide rankings are live as proof — and I'd love faction experts to gut-check their faction.
contentHtml:
A couple of weeks ago I shared a progress report on how much of 11th edition Fatecaster actually modeled: 63% of damage-affecting rules fully modeled, 93% at least partially, and a build list of the hard cases that were left.

That build list is now cleared. Every damage-affecting rule in 11th edition is modeled and ready to use. Unit abilities, army rules, detachment rules, stratagems — if a rule changes the attack math, the engine is accounting for it. The edition is in.

I want to be straight about what "modeled" means, because trust in the numbers is the whole point. A handful of rules are still deliberately approximated — narrow sub-clauses I've judged either too rare or too situational to move a real result (we still don't care whether a Guard unit "has orders", for instance). Where that's true, it's a conscious modeling decision, not a gap. The core effect of every rule that bends the dice is in the math.

The proof: edition-wide rankings

Talk is cheap, so here's the receipt. I've taken the finished engine and run it across the entire 11th edition catalog to produce a single, edition-wide power ranking of every unit.

See it live

The rankings are up right now at fatecaster.io/rankings — no login needed to look.

This only exists because the modeling is complete. You can't honestly rank a Custodes Caladius against a Tyranid Exocrine if half of either faction's rules are missing from the math. Ranking the whole edition at once is the thing that only became possible once the coverage hit 100% — which is exactly why it's the proof I wanted to ship alongside this milestone.

Where you come in

I'd genuinely love for the community to kick the tires on this — especially faction experts.

You know your faction's quirks better than any audit agent does. So my one ask: open your faction's ranking and give it a once-over. Does the order feel right? Is a unit ranked way above or below where your table experience puts it? That gut-check from someone who actually plays the faction is worth more than another pass from me.

And if something looks off, I made it easy to tell me. Open any unit in the rankings and hit the Report issue button — it flags that exact unit for my review, with the context attached, so I can dig into whether it's a modeling bug or just a surprising-but-correct result. Both outcomes are useful; sometimes the math is right and the surprise is the insight.

This is open beta, the rankings are free to browse, and the whole thing gets better the more eyes are on it. Thanks for building — and now sanity-checking — your lists with me.

title: Toward 11th Edition: Rule Coverage Progress
summary: A 15 June progress report on our modeling coverage of 11th-edition stratagems, rules, and abilities.
contentHtml:
As Warhammer 40,000 moves into its 11th edition, the question I get most is how much of the new rules does Fatecaster actually model? To answer it with real numbers rather than a hand-wave, I built a multi-agent workflow that audits the entire catalog — every unit ability, army rule, detachment rule, and stratagem — against our live damage engine. The latest pass, run on 15 June, looked at more than 6,800 distinct rules and abilities across 43 factions and 133 detachments. Of those, about 2,880 actually affect damage calculations; the rest govern movement, objectives, command points, and other effects that sit outside the attack math we are currently modeling.

Of the content that affects damage, 63% is fully modeled and 93% is at least partially modeled — meaning the core effect of the rule is already in the math, even where a narrow sub-clause may be intentionally ignored, approximated, simplified or just still being refined. That leaves roughly 200 effects (about 7%) not yet modeled and sitting in the review queue. Put plainly: when you analyze a list in Fatecaster, the overwhelming majority of the rules that change the result are already being accounted for. The remaining 7% are some difficult logic cases that need manual modelling decisions.

Encouragingly, many of the partially modelled rules have not been genuine modeling gaps; most of the items are either intentionally simplified or missing some hard to model conditions that don't have a large blast radius. Just as an example, we don't care whether a guard unit "has orders" and we have not prioritized modeling effects against units below starting strength.

How can you help?

Load up your roster and take a look at the Units and Rules pages. Every rule shows how they are modelled. If you see something off about a rule or your detachment, hit the "Report Issue" button. The rule gets reported as modelled wrong and its' automatically triaged and flagged for me to review. You are free to explain the issue or just leave it blank and I'll review the rule myself.

Thanks for your help and good hunting!

Chris

title: Welcome to the Fatecaster blog
subtitle: A dynamic content pipeline backed by GitHub.
summary: The first published artifact-driven post for Fatecaster.
contentHtml:
Welcome to the dynamic blog pipeline.

Publishing flow

Merge blog content here, run the artifact checks, and Fatecaster will pick it up from GitHub without a production application deploy.
